ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'chocolate cake' class (FOODON_03302050); no specific 'chocolate blackout cake' entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers the broader 'Cakes' category. No specific chocolate blackout cake code exists in FoodEx2; broader category anchor used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chocolate blackout cake or chocolate cake as a food item in the clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of a single-layer chocolate blackout cake slice is approximately 120 g, representing a standard restaurant dessert portion served with dark chocolate sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Chocolate cake, with chocolate frosting — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for chocolate cake with chocolate frosting used as the primary source. The input record explicitly cites HPB as the data source with confidence 0.95. The per_100g values from the input are preserved as reported. Note: the energy value of 42 kcal/100g is unusually low for a chocolate cake (typical range 350–450 kcal/100g); this anomaly is flagged in notes. The HPB-reported values are retained as canonical per the input provenance. USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, NDB No. 18139, 'Cake, chocolate, commercially prepared with chocolate frosting') is cited as a cross-reference for plausibility assessment.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 'Single Chocolate Blackout Cake' in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 38 is derived from the closest analogous entry: 'Chocolate cake, made from packet mix with chocolate frosting' (GI=38, glucose reference) as published in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283, and listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 38 × 10.36 × 1.2 / 100 = 4.72, rounded to 4.7. Note: the anomalously low energy value (42 kcal/100g) raises uncertainty about the carbohydrate figure; GL is calculated on the reported carbohydrate value.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.94 g/100g) with zero dietary fibre; negligible potassium (5 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of this high-sugar, fibre-free dessert, as excess dietary sugar promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and elevated blood pressure per AHA 2018 guidelines; sodium content itself is low at 3 mg/100g but the sugar load and absence of potassium provide no cardiovascular benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar comprises 96% of total carbohydrate (9.94 g sugar / 10.36 g carbohydrate) with zero dietary fibre to moderate glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dessert because virtually all carbohydrate is free sugar with no fibre buffer, producing a rapid postprandial glucose rise; if consumed, restrict to a very small portion and pair with a protein-containing food to attenuate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free-sugar content with no fibre, potassium, or cardioprotective micronutrients; saturated fat data unavailable but butter-based cake formulation implies saturated fat presence
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional indulgence only, given its high free-sugar content and likely saturated fat contribution from butter and chocolate, both of which are associated with adverse lipid profiles per ACC/AHA 2019 dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Single Chocolate Blackout Cake is a Western-style single-layer chocolate cake served with dark chocolate sauce, without ice cream. The 'blackout' style originates from Brooklyn, New York, where it was popularised by Ebinger's Bakery and is characterised by an intensely dark chocolate sponge layered and coated with rich chocolate pudding or ganache. In this restaurant version, a single layer of the classic cake is plated with a dark chocolate sauce accompaniment. The texture is dense and moist with a deep cocoa flavour; the sauce adds a glossy, bittersweet finish. Nutritionally, the HPB-reported values show 42 kcal, 10.36 g carbohydrate, and 9.94 g sugar per 100 g, with negligible protein (0 g) and very low fat (0.25 g/100g). These values are anomalously low compared to typical chocolate cake benchmarks (350–450 kcal/100g per USDA SR Legacy), suggesting the HPB entry may reflect a diluted or sauce-only component, or a data entry anomaly; consumers and clinicians should verify serving-size accuracy. The sugar-to-fibre ratio is extremely unfavourable (9.94 g sugar, 0 g fibre), making this unsuitable for diabetes or hypertension management. It is classified as a processed dessert, contains gluten, dairy, and eggs, and is not suitable for vegan or gluten-free diets. Typical serving size in a restaurant context is approximately 120 g per slice.

confidence_reason: The input record cites HPB Singapore as the data source with confidence 0.95 and review_status 'reviewed'. However, the reported energy value of 42 kcal/100g is highly anomalous for a chocolate cake (USDA SR Legacy reports approximately 371 kcal/100g for chocolate cake with chocolate frosting). This discrepancy raises material doubt about whether the HPB entry represents the whole cake, a sauce component, or contains a data entry error. The HPB values are preserved as reported per source policy, but confidence is downgraded to medium due to this plausibility concern.
notes: ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): protein_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g per HPB Singapore database. This is confirmed zero per the cited HPB source; however, it is nutritionally implausible for a wheat-flour and egg-based cake (USDA SR Legacy reports approximately 4.5 g protein/100g for comparable chocolate cake). The zero protein value is retained as reported by HPB but flagged as a likely data anomaly — confirmed zero per HPB source as cited. Fat_g is 0.25 g/100g, which is also anomalously low for a butter-based cake. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (g3): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for this item, and are not available in the input record. USDA FoodData Central (NDB 18139) was consulted as a cross-reference but values are not substituted as the primary source is HPB per input provenance. ENERGY ANOMALY: The reported energy of 42 kcal/100g is approximately 9-fold lower than USDA SR Legacy values for comparable chocolate cake (371 kcal/100g). This may indicate the HPB entry covers only the sauce component, a diluted preparation, or contains a data entry error. Downstream users should verify serving size and preparation basis before clinical or dietary use. HALAL STATUS: Not confirmed; flagged as false pending certification verification. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn FOODON_03302050 ('chocolate cake') used as closest_parent match; FoodEx2 A00LC ('Cakes') used as closest_parent category anchor. No invented codes used.
